Q1: I tried to keep a fairly consistent study routine, dedicating around 30 hours each week spread across most days. Being a visual learner, I'd block out time to watch lecture videos and create diagrams or mind maps from the material. I usually studied in the mornings when my mind felt clearest, though I'll admit my anxiety sometimes made it hard to stick perfectly to my schedule. I kept a simple planner where I'd write down what needed to be done each week.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but deadlines definitely shifted things around. I'd try to maintain my morning study blocks, but when assignments were due, I'd find myself putting in extra hours in the evenings too. With my low resources, I couldn't always control when I had access to a quiet space or reliable internet, so sometimes I had to adapt. The anxiety I deal with also meant some weeks I was more productive than others, even if I was technically sitting with my materials.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check if new materials had been posted or to watch the video lectures since those were most helpful for me. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts, and I'd want to see what my classmates were saying. Honestly, there were also times I logged in just to reassure myself I wasn't missing anything important—my anxiety made me worry about falling behind even when I was doing okay.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, have my tea, and log in to see what was new before starting my actual studying. It became part of my daily ritual, like checking the weather. I think having those 26 logins shows how regularly I was engaging, even if I wasn't always attending live sessions.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched them multiple times and they really helped concepts click visually. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly useful because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me new perspectives. The visual diagrams and any infographics posted were gold for my learning style. I spent a lot of time with those resources, probably around 73 minutes per video session on average.
Q6: I tended to ignore the reading-heavy PDFs that were just walls of text without any visual elements. They felt overwhelming and my brain just wouldn't absorb information that way. Some of the optional supplementary materials I'd skip too, especially when I was feeling anxious and just needed to focus on the essentials. I also didn't use the live chat features much because real-time interaction sometimes spiked my anxiety.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. Visual content that I could actually learn from efficiently was always worthwhile to me. I also valued activities where I could work at my own pace without pressure—that helped manage my anxiety. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd struggle to justify spending my limited energy on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford mentally. With my anxiety being what it is, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, so required work always came first. Sometimes optional activities actually caused me more stress because I'd worry about whether I should be doing them. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because it was visual and genuinely interesting, not because I felt obligated.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them on my phone when my internet was spotty, or I'd print out slides at the library to annotate by hand. I created physical flashcards with diagrams and color-coding that I'd study away from any screen. My notebooks are full of hand-drawn concept maps that represent hours of learning the LMS never saw.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd sit with my printed materials and handwritten notes, reviewing everything without touching my computer. I'd also study with a classmate sometimes, quizzing each other from our notes—that peer interaction wouldn't show up anywhere. When I was preparing for exams, I'd spend hours with my visual summaries spread across my desk, deeply engaged but completely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach for video content. I'd watch a lecture once online, take screenshots of key diagrams, then study those images offline repeatedly. Sometimes I'd access a PDF once to print it, and then all my actual engagement with that material happened on paper. My limited resources meant I couldn't always stay online, so downloading and working offline was essential for me.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt almost impossible. Sometimes it was triggered by an approaching deadline or feedback I was worried about receiving. During those times, I might log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. The CBT sessions I attended helped me develop some coping strategies, but there were still difficult stretches where I had to push through significant mental resistance.
Q13: My anxiety definitely affected my LMS usage—when it was high, I'd sometimes avoid logging in because I was afraid of seeing bad news or feeling overwhelmed. Fatigue was also a factor; studying 31 hours a week while managing limited resources is exhausting. There were weeks when competing responsibilities, like work or family obligations, meant I'd only do the absolute minimum online even though I was still studying offline. Confusion about certain topics would sometimes make me retreat to my own notes rather than face the LMS.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely, consumed by worry. I might scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open materials late at night when I was too tired to actually process anything meaningful. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was present but not really there.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts sometimes because the thought of putting my thoughts out there publicly made me anxious. Even though I knew participation was important, I'd wait until I felt more confident about what to say. I also avoided checking my grades immediately after submissions—I'd need to mentally prepare myself first. The CBT techniques helped me recognize this avoidance pattern, but it didn't disappear completely.
Q16: Deadlines were powerful motivators that could override my anxiety and push me into action. I'd notice my LMS activity spike significantly in the days before something was due. Having clear deadlines actually helped structure my time and gave me permission to focus intensely. Without them, I might have spread my effort too thin or gotten lost in perfectionist loops.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me—I wanted it but also dreaded it because of my anxiety. Positive feedback would boost my engagement and make me more willing to participate in discussions. Critical feedback sometimes made me withdraw temporarily while I processed it emotionally. The CBT sessions helped me learn to receive feedback more constructively, which improved my engagement over time.
Q18: The course structure with its regular posting schedule helped me establish my routine, which I appreciated. Having clear modules with defined outcomes made it easier to organize my visual study materials. However, the emphasis on attendance was challenging for me—my 22% attendance rate doesn't reflect my actual engagement with the content. I engaged deeply with the asynchronous materials even when I couldn't make synchronous sessions.
Q19: The video content and visual resources made engagement much easier for me given my learning style. Having materials available to download was crucial given my internet limitations. What made things harder was when important information was only shared in live sessions I couldn't attend. Also, some activities required quick responses which triggered my anxiety about saying the wrong thing.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to engage but struggling with the material or feeling overwhelmed. They might be logging in, watching videos, reading posts—all that activity—but then freeze when it comes to actually submitting work. For someone with anxiety like me, there's a big difference between consuming content and putting yourself out there with a submission. It could also be someone who's learning a lot but doesn't trust their understanding enough to commit it to paper.
Q21: This actually resonates with my experience quite a bit. My exam score of nearly 85% and my Merit grade don't necessarily match what my attendance or some of my LMS metrics might suggest. I did a lot of learning offline with my visual materials, printed notes, and hand-drawn diagrams. Someone looking at raw activity data might underestimate how much genuine learning was happening away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and how long they spend on pages. They can track submission times and participation in discussions. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during online time, and the emotional state of the student. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing while staring at the wall anxiously.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material in a way that leads to understanding and growth. For me, it's when I'm actively processing information, creating visual representations, and making connections between concepts. It's not just about time spent or clicks made—it's about mental presence and authentic learning. True engagement also involves participating in the learning community, even when that's scary for someone with anxiety.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with my hand-drawn concept maps and color-coded notes spread across my floor. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I pushed through to submit assignments or the relief I felt when I finally understood something. It can't measure how the CBT sessions helped me become a more resilient learner over time. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite my struggles with attendance and anxiety, I was genuinely committed to learning and proud of earning my Merit grade.
